## II. The Underlying Axes

::: aside
**META:** Load-bearing. Without this, archetypes arrive as a gallery without a scaffold. Keep tightly scoped — only axes that are (a) genuinely cross-cutting and (b) not immediately obvious to a LW reader. These are the dimensions that, once named, make the portrait comparisons legible.
:::

Five dimensions do most of the structural work in this debate. They're not the only axes, but they're the ones where positions are genuinely independent of each other — you can be anywhere on Axis 2 regardless of where you are on Axis 5. Getting these clear first makes the threat models below legible in ways they otherwise wouldn't be.

**Axis 1 — What system is actually dangerous?**
Is the concern about AI systems that exist now, AI that will exist soon in the current paradigm, or a qualitatively different class of system that hasn't been built yet? This axis separates three positions: the Present-Harms Realist (harm is already here, at scale), the Capability Skeptic (the genuinely dangerous system hasn't been built and won't emerge from scaling LLMs), and the Capability Extrapolationist (something qualitatively different is coming, and current benchmarks are largely irrelevant). Your position on this axis determines which evidence is even worth arguing about.

**Axis 2 — Does dangerous agency emerge by default, or is it a design choice?**
This is the most load-bearing crux in the map. Bengio's entire research agenda rests on one answer; Yudkowsky's moratorium position rests on the other. If sufficiently capable AI systems converge on power-seeking behavior regardless of how they were built — because that's what optimization pressure does — then architectural choices don't help much and the fight is about whether you build the thing at all. If agency is a design choice that can be made or avoided, then the relevant fight is architectural, not existential.

**Axis 3 — What does the failure mechanism look like?**
Single optimizer achieving decisive strategic advantage? A competitive dynamic among many systems and actors with no one in control? Gradual erosion of meaningful human oversight through a thousand small decisions? Deliberate misuse by bad actors? Your answer determines which interventions are even in the relevant solution space — and reveals alliances that look impossible from the top level. Andrew Critch (~85% p(doom)) and Yann LeCun (~0% p(doom) from this route) both reject the unipolar optimizer story. They have almost nothing else in common.

**Axis 4 — What kind of evidence is load-bearing?**
Some people reason primarily from structural arguments — instrumental convergence, orthogonality, the difficulty of value loading — and treat current AI behavior as near-zero evidence about the relevant risk. Others update substantially on empirical observations: what current AI systems actually do, what alignment techniques show promising results, whether deployments go well or badly. These aren't just different conclusions — they're different evidence types, which means the conversations don't converge when you show them the same data. This axis predicts, more than almost anything else, whether two people in this debate can update each other at all.

**Axis 5 — What does "doom" mean, and over what window?**
Extinction? Permanent and irreversible loss of human control over humanity's future? Severe civilizational disempowerment even if people survive? The word carries enormous weight in this debate while meaning entirely different things to different people. Critch's 85% and Yudkowsky's ~99% look numerically adjacent until you notice they're not talking about the same endpoint.

But there's a second dimension that rarely gets named: the time window. Amodei's concern focuses on misuse risks in the next 5–10 years. Ord's risk estimates in *The Precipice* are framed over centuries. These aren't adjacent points on the same axis — they're different forecasts with different urgency profiles and different intervention windows. A 20% p(doom)-over-5-years and a 20% p(doom)-over-100-years would imply radically different priorities even if the final numbers matched. Most stated p(doom) numbers don't specify the window, which makes comparison extremely difficult. This isn't a semantic quibble. Different time windows imply different success conditions — and radically different intervention priorities.
